mnemonics.title: Process States Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: NEW TO READY RUNNING WAITING TERMINATED - THINK OF A FACTORY: NEW (raw material), READY (in queue), RUNNING (being processed), WAITING (waiting for parts), TERMINATED (finished product)

mnemonics.title: Scheduling Criteria
mnemonics.content: CPU UTILIZATION + THROUGHPUT = EFFICIENCY; TURNAROUND + WAITING + RESPONSE = SPEED. REMEMBER: UTILITY AND SPEED ARE KEY.

mnemonics.title: Thread Models
mnemonics.content: MANY-TO-ONE = ONE LANE (one kernel thread for all user threads); ONE-TO-ONE = ONE LANE PER CAR (one kernel per user); MANY-TO-MANY = HIGHWAY (flexible mapping)

mnemonics.title: Critical Section Conditions
mnemonics.content: MUTUAL EXCLUSION = NO SHARING; PROGRESS = NO DELAY; BOUNDED WAITING = FAIRNESS. THINK: MPB FOR SHORT.

acronyms.expansion: Process Control Block - stores all process information
acronyms.usage: Used by OS to manage and track each process in the system

acronyms.expansion: Inter-Process Communication - methods for processes to exchange data
acronyms.usage: Used when multiple processes need to share information or coordinate actions

acronyms.expansion: First-Come-First-Served - scheduling algorithm based on arrival time
acronyms.usage: Simplest CPU scheduling algorithm, processes execute in order of arrival

acronyms.expansion: Shortest Job First - scheduling algorithm based on burst time
acronyms.usage: Minimizes average waiting time, but may cause starvation

acronyms.expansion: Round Robin - time-slice based preemptive scheduling
acronyms.usage: Fair scheduling where each process gets equal CPU time quantum

acronyms.expansion: Process Identifier - unique ID assigned to each process
acronyms.usage: Used by OS to identify and manage processes uniquely

acronyms.expansion: Thread Identifier - unique ID for each thread
acronyms.usage: Used to identify and manage individual threads within a process

visualTips.visualization: DRAW A CIRCLE WITH 5 BOXES: NEW→READY (admitted), READY→RUNNING (dispatch), RUNNING→WAITING (I/O request), WAITING→READY (I/O complete), RUNNING→READY (preempt). THIS SHOWS THE LIFECYCLE.
THINK OF TWO PEOPLE SHARING ONE NOTEBOOK: SAVE CURRENT PERSON'S WORK (registers, PC) → GIVE NOTEBOOK TO NEXT PERSON → LOAD THEIR SAVED WORK. THIS IS CONTEXT SWITCHING.
DRAW THREE QUEUES: JOB QUEUE (disk), READY QUEUE (memory), DEVICE QUEUE (per I/O device). PROCESSES MOVE BETWEEN THESE QUEUES.
DRAW A HOUSE (PROCESS) WITH MULTIPLE ROOMS (THREADS). EACH ROOM HAS ITS OWN DESK (STACK) BUT SHARES THE KITCHEN, LIVING ROOM (CODE, DATA, HEAP).

analogies.analogy: PROCESS IS LIKE A FACTORY BUILDING, THREAD IS LIKE A WORKER IN THAT FACTORY. MULTIPLE WORKERS SHARE THE SAME BUILDING RESOURCES BUT HAVE THEIR OWN TOOLS.
LONG-TERM SCHEDULER IS LIKE DECIDING HOW MANY CARS TO LET ONTO THE HIGHWAY, SHORT-TERM IS LIKE DIRECTING CARS AT AN INTERSECTION.
CONTEXT SWITCHING IS LIKE A TEACHER SWITCHING BETWEEN STUDENTS: SAVE ONE STUDENT'S ANSWER, CALL NEXT STUDENT, START WHERE THEY LEFT OFF.
INTER-PROCESS COMMUNICATION IS LIKE AN OFFICE INTERCOM: ONE PERSON SPEAKS INTO IT, OTHERS HEAR THE MESSAGE (MESSAGE PASSING) OR THERE'S A SHARED BULLETIN BOARD (SHARED MEMORY).
